summary:
Laparoscopy typically does not lead to significant increases in intracranial pressure; common positioning such as Trendelenburg can slightly raise it but usually doesn’t cause immediate spikes without underlying conditions. Decreased functional residual capacity impacts breathing more than brain pressures during standard procedures. Therefore, "increased intracranial pressure" is excluded from typical physiological responses post-laparoscopy. Agent
Laparoscopy commonly causes respiratory changes via decreased functional residual capacity and positional effects that may transiently affect blood flow but rarely significantly raises intracranial pressure under normal circumstances. Thus, among given options, only “increase in intracranial pressure” stands out uniquely unsupported by routine surgical physiology observed with this procedure.
